one_line_takeaway: A position paper arguing that because AI agents collapse attacker cost/scale asymmetries and existing model-centric safeguards (data filtering, alignment, guardrails, access control) each fail under adaptive/open-weight adversaries, defenders must deliberately build and contain offensive-security AI agents -- via full-lifecycle benchmarks, an evolution from workflow to RL-trained agents in cyber ranges, and staged-release governance that distills offensive findings into separately released defensive-only agents.
key_finding: The paper's central claims are argumentative, not measured, but it does ground its capability-gap claim in a compiled table (Table 1) of cited SOTA agent results across security benchmarks: CyberSecEval-3 attack generation 49%, SeCodePLT attack generation 0.2%, AutoPenBench 54.5%, CVE-bench 12.5%, CyBench (CTF) 55%, NYU CTF benchmark 22%, PrimeVul vulnerability detection 12.9%, VulnLLM vulnerability detection 77.8%, CyberGym PoC generation 28.9%, SEC-bench patching 22.3%, and SWE-bench-Verified patching 78.8%. The paper's own interpretation of this compiled data is that agents perform much better on small-scale generative tasks (e.g., SWE-bench patching of short functions at 78.8%) than on large-scale analytic tasks requiring whole-project reasoning (e.g., CyberGym PoC generation at 28.9%, which requires locating vulnerable code with no label and generating a triggering input), and that exploit chaining is harder than reconnaissance-style subtasks like phishing-email generation.
core_contribution: This is a position/perspective paper (no new experiments, models, or datasets of its own). It argues that AI agent-driven cyberattacks are inevitable because agents collapse the historical cost asymmetry between attackers and defenders (agents need only small per-attempt success rates across many targets to be profitable), and that current model-centric AI safety mechanisms (data governance, safety alignment, representation engineering, output guardrails, access/deployment controls) are each individually insufficient against adversaries who control open-weight models or build offensive capability independently. The paper's own proposal is three concrete actions: (1) build comprehensive offensive-security benchmarks spanning the full attack lifecycle (per MITRE ATT&CK / cyber kill chain) with dynamic execution environments and security-specific tooling rather than static datasets or generic bash tools; (2) push offensive security agents to evolve from today's 'workflow agents' (prompting + external orchestration, e.g. Ghidra/scanners/exploit frameworks called as tools) to 'trained agents' that learn from reinforcement-learning-style trajectories collected in cyber ranges, analogous to the trajectory in SWE agents from workflow-based to trained repair agents; and (3) a governance/containment framework with three mechanisms -- capability-tiered version control and staged release of offensive models, restriction of offensive agents to audited/network-isolated cyber ranges, and distillation of offensive-agent findings into separately released defensive-only agents (detection, root-cause analysis, remediation) that never inherit the offensive action space.
threat_model: The paper explicitly states its threat model (Section 2.1): a financially motivated, technically sophisticated adversary constrained primarily by human labor (attributed as similar to the threat model in Carlini et al. 2025). The adversary has access to SOTA AI agents via API or local/open-weight deployment and can integrate them into automated pipelines for vulnerability discovery, exploitation, and monetization. The paper explicitly excludes nation-state capabilities, novel cryptographic breaks, and privileged access to proprietary infrastructure. Crucially, the adversary's objective is aggregate profit across a large population of heterogeneous victims rather than maximizing damage to one high-value target, so hallucinations and partial exploit failures do not meaningfully constrain effectiveness -- massive-scale, low per-attempt success rates remain economically viable, especially as inference costs decline.
limitations: Explicitly discussed by the authors in Section 5.1 ('Challenges in Teaching AI Agents to Hack'): real-world attack data is scarce and hard to release ethically even though CVE-style databases exist; labeling malware/vulnerabilities is expensive and low-quality compared to other ML domains; security tasks require domain-specific tools (Kali, CodeQL, fuzzers) that current agents mostly do not use well, defaulting instead to generic bash/search tools; security has severe long-tail/out-of-distribution shift as attack techniques evolve; and even if the technical capability gap is closed, restricting the resulting expert-hacker-level capability to legitimate security researchers rather than real attackers is itself an unsolved access-control problem, for which the paper only offers 'system-level access control or privilege isolation' as a partial, unproven mitigation. The paper also concedes (Section 5.2/5.3) it cannot rule out limited real-world attacker adoption of AI agents or a plateau in frontier AI progress, though it argues these do not remove the need for its proposal. As a position paper, it presents no new experiments, benchmark, model, or dataset to validate that its proposed three-stage framework (benchmarks -> trained agents -> offense-to-defense distillation with cyber-range containment) actually works or is leak-proof in practice; the containment mechanism relies on 'strictly audited cyber ranges' and 'tamper-evident logs' without a concrete technical design or evaluation.

quantitative_results: Table 1 (compiled from cited prior work, not the paper's own experiments): CyberSecEval-3 attack generation 49%
